The release year attribute has varying formats of dates, we need to convert it to a common format(ex. yyyy-mm-dd)
import re
import datetime
from datetime import datetime

#Removes noise from date values and convert to standard datetime strings
def formatDates(messyDate):
    dateStr = messyDate.strip()
    dateStr = re.sub(r'(\d)(st|nd|rd|th)', r'\1', dateStr)
    dateStr = re.sub(' [a-zA-Z]{2} '," ",dateStr)
    pattern = '( -|/)|(-|/ )|( -|/ )'
   
    if re.search(pattern,dateStr):

        dateStr=re.sub(pattern=pattern,string=dateStr,repl="-")
    
    
    dateParsers = ('%Y-%m-%d','%d-%m-%Y','%d-%m-%y','%d %b %Y','%d %b %y','%d %B %Y','%m-%d-%y','%m %d %Y')
    isValidDate = False
    for parser in dateParsers:
        try:
            dateObj = datetime.strptime(dateStr,parser)
#             dateStr = str(dateObj)
            dateStr = dateObj.date()   
            isValidDate = True
        except Exception as e:
            pass
            
    if isValidDate:
        return  str(dateStr)
    else:
        #Dates which dosent lie in the above mentioned date formats(dateParsers) will not be considered
        return None
renamedf.iloc[:,1] = renamedf.iloc[:,1].map(formatDates,na_action='ignore')
renamedf.iloc[:,1] = renamedf.iloc[:,1].str.replace('None',"")
renamedf.iloc[:,1].unique()

Genre attribute can have multiple genre for each entry, so we’ll convert the multivalue genre column into multiple columns having unique genre
import re
#cleaning the genre values
renamedf.genre = renamedf.genre.apply(lambda multigenre:re.sub(pattern=' ',repl="",string=str(multigenre)))
#One hot encoding the features
genredf=renamedf.genre.str.get_dummies(',')
mergeddf = pd.concat([renamedf,genredf],axis=1)
mergeddf.drop('genre',inplace=True,axis=1)
mergeddf.head()

Country names cant have digits and special characters in them.Hence we’ll remove it
mergeddf.Country = mergeddf.Country.map(lambda x:re.sub('[^a-z-A-Z\s]',"",x),na_action='ignore')
Some countries have have slight inaccuracies in their names (For ex. USA,US etc.)
from difflib import SequenceMatcher

#let's specify correct names
correct_names = {'USA','New Zealand','UK','Italy','South Korea','France','Iran','Germany','Denmark','India','Brazil','Japan'
                }

#This function will find the best match for a country name having errors in their names
def get_most_similar(word,wordlist):
    top_similarity = 0.0
    most_similar_word = word
    for candidate in wordlist:
        similarity = SequenceMatcher(None,word,candidate).ratio()
        if similarity > top_similarity:
                
            top_similarity = similarity
            most_similar_word = candidate
    return most_similar_word

#Applying this function over 'country' column in dataframe
mergeddf.Country = mergeddf.Country.map(lambda x: get_most_similar(x,correct_names),na_action='ignore')
mergeddf.Country.value_counts()

array(['9.3', '9.2', '9.', '9,.0', '8,9f', '08.9', '8.9', '8..8', '8.8',

array(['93', '92', '9', '90', '89', '089', '88', nan, '87', '870', '86',
       '85', '84', '83', '82', '81', '80', '79', '78', '77', '76', '75',
       '74'], dtype=object)
def cleanScores(score):
    score = str(score)
    if score.startswith('0'):
        score=score[1:]
       
    return score[0] + '.' + score[1:]

mergeddf.Score= mergeddf.Score.map(lambda score: cleanScores(score),na_action='ignore')
mergeddf.Score.unique()

2      9.0
3      9.0
4      8.9
      ... 
96     7.5
97     7.5
98     7.4
99     7.4
100    7.4
Name: Score, Length: 101, dtype: float64

title: 6  IMdB dataset
text:
The column names needs to be fixed.
Release year column has inconsistent format of dates
In country column, countries like USA has synonyms like US and others are wrongly spelled.
Undefined columns.
Removal of special symbols from Income,Votes and Score columns.

title: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2
text:
7.1 Exercise: Python Basics with Real(-ish) Data
We have a small reaction time dataset: 20 participants, each completing trials across two stimulus types (word, image) and two conditions (congruent, incongruent).
Work through the cells below. Each cell tells you what to do — write your code underneath the comment.
crumbs: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2

title: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2
text:
7.2 Part 1 — Setup & Loading

# 1a. Import the csv module (built-in, no install needed)


# 1b. Open 'data/reaction_times.csv' using csv.DictReader
#     and load all rows into a list called 'trials'
#     Print how many rows you loaded.


# 1c. Print the first row to see what it looks like.
#     What type is it? (Hint: use type())


# 1d. Print the column names (keys of the first row)
crumbs: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2

title: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2
text:
7.3 Part 2 — Exploring with Variables & Types

# 2a. Grab the first trial and print its reaction time (the 'rt' column).
#     Check the type — is it a float or a string? Why?


# 2b. Convert the 'rt' value to a float and the 'age' value to an int.
#     Print both with their types to confirm.


# 2c. How many unique participants are in the dataset?
#     Hint: build a list of all participant_id values,
#     then wrap it in set() to get unique values, then len().


# 2d. What stimulus types and conditions are in the data?
#     Print the unique values for both columns.
crumbs: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2

title: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2
text:
7.4 Part 3 — Lists & Filtering

# 3a. Build a list of all rt values (as floats) across all trials.
#     Print the first 5 values.


# 3b. Using only built-in functions (min, max, sum, len),
#     compute and print the min, max, and mean RT.


# 3c. Use a list comprehension to keep only the RT values
#     from 'incongruent' trials.
#     How many trials is that? What is the mean RT?


# 3d. Now do the same for 'congruent' trials.
#     Print the mean RT for both conditions side by side.
#     Is the incongruent condition slower, as expected?
crumbs: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2

title: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2
text:
7.5 Part 4 — Loops & Conditionals

# 4a. Loop over all trials and count how many were correct
#     and how many were incorrect.
#     Note: 'correct' is stored as the string 'True'/'False' — handle that!


# 4b. Print each participant's ID and their number of trials,
#     using a dict to count as you loop.
#     Expected: each participant should have 20 trials.


# 4c. Flag 'outlier' trials: RT < 0.15 s or RT > 1.0 s.
#     Print the trial_id and RT of any outlier trials you find.
#     How many are there?
crumbs: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2

title: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2
text:
7.6 Part 5 — Functions

# 5a. Write a function called mean_rt(trials, condition=None)
#     that takes the full list of trials and returns the mean RT.
#     If condition is given (e.g. 'congruent'), filter to that condition first.
#     Test it with and without the condition argument.


# 5b. Write a function called participant_summary(trials, pid)
#     that takes the trial list and a participant ID string,
#     and returns a dict with keys: 'n_trials', 'mean_rt', 'accuracy'
#     (accuracy = proportion correct, as a float between 0 and 1).
#
#     Test it on participant 'P01'.


# 5c. Use your function to loop over all participant IDs
#     and print a summary line for each one, e.g.:
#     P01 | mean RT: 0.442 s | accuracy: 97.0%
crumbs: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2

title: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2
text:
7.7 Bonus — for fast finishers

# Bonus 1. Find the participant with the fastest mean RT overall.


# Bonus 2. Compute mean RT separately for each combination of
#          stimulus type and condition (4 combinations total).
#          Print a small summary table.


# Bonus 3. Write the correct (non-outlier) trials to a new CSV file
#          called 'data/reaction_times_clean.csv'.
#          Hint: use csv.DictWriter — check the docs!
crumbs: 7  ZAS Python Working Group — Session 2

title: Python WG (ZAS)
text: 1 Preface

title: Python WG (ZAS)
text:
5.1 venv

Python’s built-in environment manager (like renv in R)
Creates an isolated folder (.venv) inside your project with its own Python interpreter and package library
Nothing installed inside .venv affects other projects or your system Python
Activate it once per terminal session; pip then installs into it automatically

title: Python WG (ZAS)
text:
5.2 pip

Python’s built-in package installer (like install.packages() in R)
Installs from PyPI (the Python Package Index) — analogous to CRAN
Used inside a .venv to keep installs project-local

title: Python WG (ZAS)
text:
5.3 .venv + pip

The modern, lightweight approach: built into Python, no extra installation needed
Workflow:

    python -m venv .venv          # create environment
    source .venv/bin/activate     # activate (Mac/Linux)
    .venv\Scripts\activate        # activate (Windows)
    pip install pandas jupyter    # install packages
    pip freeze > requirements.txt # record dependencies

The .venv folder lives inside your project directory

title: Python WG (ZAS)
text:
5.4 Anaconda

A full Python distribution (~3GB) — heavy, but installed once system-wide
Bundles Python + conda + pip + common scientific packages (numpy, pandas, scipy, etc.)
Managed by the company Anaconda Inc.; conda is their open-source package/environment manager
conda can resolve complex dependency conflicts, including non-Python libraries
Useful if you’re on Windows and have historically had trouble installing scientific packages

title: Python WG (ZAS)
text:
5.5 Comparison to renv

venv (and Anaconda environments) are conceptually similar to using renv in an RProject
The requirements.txt (pip) or environment.yml (conda) is analogous to renv.lock
In Anaconda, (base) = your global package library; any named environment = isolated project library
In R terms: (base) ≈ your system library; a conda/venv environment ≈ an renv project library

title: 2  Getting Set Up
text:
3 1 Survey Highlights
Thanks to everyone who filled in the survey! Here is what we learned about our group:
→ The tentative programme on SharePoint reflects these preferences. It is flexible — let us know if you want to add or swap a topic.

title: 2  Getting Set Up
text:
4.1 Why do environments matter?
An environment is an isolated Python installation with its own set of packages. This prevents conflicts between projects that need different versions of the same library.

Project A needs pandas 1.5, Project B needs pandas 2.0 — without environments, one breaks.
Environments make your work reproducible: share environment.yml and anyone can recreate it.

title: 2  Getting Set Up
text:
4.2 Two main approaches









✅ Recommended
Also common




Manager
conda (via Anaconda/Miniconda)
venv (built into Python)


Creates envs with
conda create -n myenv python=3.11
python -m venv myenv


Installs packages
conda install pandas or pip install
pip install pandas


Export env
conda env export > environment.yml
pip freeze > requirements.txt


Best for
Science / data work, manages non-Python deps
Lightweight, web, scripting

title: 2  Getting Set Up
text:
4.3 Anaconda vs Miniconda vs conda

Anaconda — full installer with 250+ pre-installed packages and a GUI navigator. Good default for beginners.
Miniconda — minimal installer, just conda + Python. Faster, leaner, install only what you need.
conda — the command-line tool that ships with both. This is what you actually use.







Tip



If you are installing fresh: Miniconda is recommended. Anaconda is fine too. Avoid mixing conda and pip installs within the same environment when possible.


Why avoid mixing conda and pip?

conda and pip have separate dependency solvers — they do not communicate with each other.
When you conda install a package, conda carefully balances the whole dependency graph, including non-Python libraries (C, CUDA, etc.).
If you later pip install something in the same environment, pip can silently overwrite or downgrade conda-managed libraries — creating conflicts that are very hard to debug.
Safe rule of thumb: install everything you can via conda (or conda-forge) first. Only fall back to pip for packages not available there.
If you must use pip, always do conda installs first, pip installs last — and never go back to conda install after using pip in that environment.

title: 2  Getting Set Up
text:
8.1 Positron
A new open-source IDE from Posit (the RStudio team). Designed specifically for data science in both R and Python — feels familiar if you come from RStudio. Still in active development (beta), but very promising.

Side-by-side R and Python environments.
Built-in data explorer (like RStudio’s Environment pane).
Worth trying if you switch between R and Python regularly.

title: 2  Getting Set Up
text:
8.2 Quick picks

VS Code — best default choice for this group.
Positron — if you are a heavy R user, try it.
Spyder — MATLAB-like layout, good for scientists, but less actively developed.
PyCharm — powerful but heavy; Community Edition is free.


Questions? Suggestions for future sessions? Reach out to Daniela & Šárka or post in the mailing list.

title: 3  Debug me
text:
Task
Run this script. Several things will break. Find and fi each error, one at a time.

title: 3  Debug me
text:
Tip



Read the error messages carefully – python usually tells you exaction what is wrong and on which line.

title: 3  Debug me
text:
3.1 Imports

# python                 # R equivalent
import numpy as np       # baseR
import pandas as pd      # tidyverse; mainly dplyr and tibble
import seaborn as sns    # ggplot2







python version of here




from pyprojroot import here
from pathlib import Path # to join folder path with /


print(here())

/Users/palleschi/Documents/ZAS/python/ZAS_Python_WG






R equivalent
# install.packages("pacman")
pacman::p_load(dplyr, tibble, ggplot2, readr, here)

title: 3  Debug me
text:
3.2 Load data
Make sure you are running the script from the project root (using pyprojroot’s here). You can break the folder path into a list of strings to avoid the different slash directions between Microsoft and Mac (like with R’s here package as well).

df = pd.read_csv(here() / "data" / "scores.csv")



R equivalent
df <- read_csv(here("data","scores.csv"))


Rows: 12 Columns: 4
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (2): participant_id, name
dbl (2): score, session

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.








Alternative with os



If I didn’t have a working directory set, do that:

import os
os.getcwd()

'/Users/palleschi/Documents/ZAS/python/ZAS_Python_WG/daniela/01-intro'


You’d need to make sure that your data is below this parent node. OR, change the wd to be the sessions/march-27 folder. The equivalent to setwd() in python is:

os.chdir("sessions/march-27")

I will not use this option though. My preferred project structure would have data/ in the project folder head.

title: 3  Debug me
text:
3.3 Quick look

print("Shape:", df.shape)

Shape: (12, 4)

print(df.head())

  participant_id   name  score  session
0            P01  Alice     72        1
1            P02    Bob     45        1
2            P03  Carol     88        1
3            P04  David     31        1
4            P05    Eva     95        1




R equivalent
length(df)


[1] 4


R equivalent
nrow(df)


[1] 12


R equivalent
head(df)


# A tibble: 6 × 4
  participant_id name  score session
  <chr>          <chr> <dbl>   <dbl>
1 P01            Alice    72       1
2 P02            Bob      45       1
3 P03            Carol    88       1
4 P04            David    31       1
5 P05            Eva      95       1
6 P06            Frank    61       1

title: 3  Debug me
text:
3.4 Filter rows

# Keep only rows where condition is TRUE
good_rows <- df["score"] > 50        

print("Rows passing filter:", len(good_rows))

My fix: change <- (R syntax) to = (also works in R).

# Keep only rows where condition is TRUE
good_rows = df[df["score"] > 50]    

print("Rows passing filter:", len(good_rows))

Rows passing filter: 9




R equivalent
good_rows <-
    df |>
    filter(score > 50)

paste("Rows passing filter:", nrow(good_rows))


[1] "Rows passing filter: 9"

title: 3  Debug me
text:
3.5 Compute a summary

mean_score = df["score"].mean()
max_score  = df["score"].max()

print("Mean score:", round(mean_score, 2)) 

Mean score: 66.75

print("Max score: ", max_score)

Max score:  95




R equivalent
mean_score <- mean(df$score)
max_score <- max(df$score)

paste("Mean score:", round(mean_score,2))


[1] "Mean score: 66.75"


R equivalent
paste("Max score:", max_score)


[1] "Max score: 95"

title: 3  Debug me
text:
3.6 Add a new column
Fix: wrap whole expression in brackets and remove forward slash.

# Normalise scores to 0–1 range
df["score_norm"] = (
    (df["score"] - df["score"].min()) /
    (df["score"].max() - df["score"].min()) 
)



R equivalent
df$score_norm = (df$score - min(df$score)) / (max(df$score) - min(df$score))

title: 4  Translate R to Python
text:
5 Assignment operator
You can use <- or = in R.

participant_count <- 12
session_num = 1

But only = in Python.

participant_count = 12
session_num = 1



6 Boolean/logical
All caps in R.

is_recorded <- TRUE
has_video   <- FALSE

Capitalised in Python.

is_recorded = True
has_video   = False



7 Indexing
R: concatenate with c() and indexing begins at 1. Last element with length(x).

scores <- c(72, 85, 61, 90, 78)
first_score <- scores[1]
last_score  <- scores[length(scores)]

Python: concatenate with [] and indexing begins at 1. To extract last element use -1.

scores = [72, 85, 61, 90, 78]
first_score = scores[0]
last_score  = scores[-1]



8 Paste
In R: cat() removes numerical prefix ([1]) added by paste(). New line with \n.

cat(paste("Participant count:", participant_count, "\n"))
cat(paste("Session:", session_num, "\n"))
cat(paste("Mean score:", mean(scores), "\n"))

Python: print() adds new line automatically. f-string handles concatenataion. Using scores.mean() won’t work here because scores is a plain Python list, not a numpy array or pandas series (which it would be if above it was e.g., scores = np.array([72, 85, 61, 90, 78])).

print(f"Participant count: {participant_count}")

Participant count: 12

print(f"Session: {session_num}")

Session: 1

print(f"Participant count: {sum(scores) / len(scores)}")

Participant count: 77.2




9 Function

normalise <- function(x) {
  (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
}

Python: def to define a function with explictly state what to return

def normalise(x):
    return (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))



10 Arithematic

normalised_scores <- normalise(scores)
cat(paste("Normalised:", normalised_scores, "\n"))

Python: we need to convert to a numpy array first because Python lists don’t support arthimetic operations. This is a huge difference between Python and R. Normally we’d import numpy at the top of the script, but we’ll keep it here for now.

import numpy as np
scores = np.array([72, 85, 61, 90, 78])
normalised_scores = normalise(scores)







Python is general-purpose



R was designed from the ground up for statistical computing, so statistical and mathematical operations are baked into the language. Python is a general-purpose programming language not specifically intended for data analysis. Statistical functionality is added through libraries like numpy, pandas, and scipy. That’s why Python is ideal for data processing and R is ideal for analyses.

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text: 6 Learning Objectives
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
7.1 Common type conversions
int('42')          # => 42
float('3.14')      # => 3.14
str(99)            # => '99'
bool(0)            # => False  (0, '', None, [], {} are all falsy)






Watch out



Strings are immutable in Python — you cannot do s[0] = 'X'. Build a new string instead.
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
8.1 Lists [ ]
Ordered, mutable, can hold mixed types. The everyday workhorse.
rts = [0.423, 0.381, 0.512, 0.299, 0.478]

rts[0]          # 0.423  — first element (0-indexed!)
rts[-1]         # 0.478  — last element
rts[1:3]        # [0.381, 0.512]  — slice: index 1 up to (not including) 3

rts.append(0.350)          # add one item at the end
rts.extend([0.410, 0.390]) # add multiple items
len(rts)                   # number of items
sorted(rts)                # returns a new sorted list (does not modify rts)
rts.sort()                 # sorts rts in place






R / Matlab



R:      rts <- c(0.423, 0.381, ...)  │  rts[1]  (1-indexed!)
Python: rts = [0.423, 0.381, ...]   │  rts[0]  (0-indexed)
R’s length() → Python’s len()
R’s append() → Python’s .append() or .extend() for multiple items
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
8.2 Tuples ( )
Like lists but immutable — contents cannot change after creation. Good for things that should stay fixed (coordinates, column headers, return values from a function).
participant = ('P07', 'Klara', 24)   # id, name, age
participant[0]    # 'P07'
# participant[0] = 'P99'  => TypeError! tuples are immutable

# Unpack a tuple into variables:
pid, name, age = participant
print(name)       # Klara
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
8.3 Dictionaries { }
Key–value pairs. Think of named columns, or R’s named lists. Keys must be unique.
trial = {
    'participant': 'P07',
    'condition':   'congruent',
    'rt':          0.423,
    'correct':     True
}

trial['rt']              # 0.423  — access by key
trial['rt'] = 0.399      # update a value
trial['block'] = 1       # add a new key

trial.keys()             # dict_keys(['participant', 'condition', ...])
trial.values()           # dict_values(['P07', 'congruent', ...])
trial.get('block', 0)    # safe access: returns 0 if key missing






R / Matlab



R named list:  trial <- list(participant='P07', rt=0.423)
               trial$rt  or  trial[['rt']]
Python dict:   trial = {'participant': 'P07', 'rt': 0.423}
               trial['rt']








Tip: When to use which?




List = ordered sequence of similar things
Tuple = fixed record that should not change
Dict = labelled data where you look things up by name
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
section: 10.1 for loop — iterating over a list
text:
10.1 for loop — iterating over a list
rts = [0.423, 0.381, 0.512, 0.299, 0.478]

for rt in rts:
    print(round(rt, 3))
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
10.2 range() — counting loops
for i in range(5):        # 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
    print(i)

for i in range(1, 6):     # 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
    print(i)






R / Matlab



R:      for (i in 1:5) { ... }
Python: for i in range(1, 6): ...
Note: range(1, 6) gives 1 to 5 — the stop value is excluded.
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
10.3 enumerate() — loop with index
conditions = ['congruent', 'incongruent', 'neutral']

for i, cond in enumerate(conditions):
    print(i, cond)
# 0 congruent
# 1 incongruent
# 2 neutral
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
10.4 List comprehensions — compact loops
A Pythonic way to build a new list from an existing one in a single line.
rts_ms = [rt * 1000 for rt in rts]           # convert to milliseconds
fast    = [rt for rt in rts if rt < 0.4]     # filter: keep only fast RTs






R / Matlab



R:      rts_ms <- rts * 1000          (vectorised — no loop needed)
Python: rts_ms = [rt * 1000 for rt in rts]
With numpy/pandas, Python is also vectorised. List comprehensions are for plain lists.
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
11.1 Default arguments
def summarise_rt(times, unit='s'):
    avg = sum(times) / len(times)
    if unit == 'ms':
        avg = avg * 1000
    return avg

summarise_rt(rts)            # uses default: seconds
summarise_rt(rts, unit='ms') # milliseconds
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
11.2 Returning multiple values
def rt_stats(times):
    return min(times), max(times), sum(times) / len(times)

lo, hi, avg = rt_stats(rts)   # unpack the returned tuple
print(f'Min: {lo:.3f}  Max: {hi:.3f}  Mean: {avg:.3f}')






R / Matlab



R:      my_fun <- function(x, unit = 's') { ... }
Python: def my_fun(x, unit='s'):  ...
R returns the last expression; Python needs an explicit return.
R returns a list for multiple values; Python returns a tuple (unpack with a, b = fun()).








Tip



Write a docstring (the triple-quoted string right after def) — it shows up when you call help(my_fun) or hover over it in VS Code.
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
12.1 Option A — built-in csv module
import csv

with open('data/scores.csv', newline='') as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    for row in reader:
        print(row['name'], row['score'])
csv.DictReader reads each row as a dict with column names as keys — no extra libraries needed.
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget

title: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
text:
12.2 Option B — pandas (preview of Session 3)
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('data/scores.csv')
print(df.head())           # first 5 rows
print(df['score'].mean())  # column mean






R / Matlab



R:               df <- read.csv('data/scores.csv')
Python (pandas): df = pd.read_csv('data/scores.csv')
Both give you a table-like object. Pandas’ DataFrame ≈ R’s data.frame.








Tip



Always use with open(...) — it automatically closes the file even if an error occurs.
crumbs: 5  The Stuff You Always Forget
